---
title: ""
output: 
  html_document: 
    theme: cosmo
    css: style.css
---

#

<center> ![A picture of me at work](me25.jpg) </center>

$$\Large\text{Bob Week}$$

<p align=justify style="background-color:#2E2E2E;
border-radius:4px;
font-size:16px;
padding:15px;
margin:5px;"><b>
I am interested in the relationship between patterns and processes shaping ecological communities. This inquiry leads to a broad range of questions involving mechanisms for species coexistence, the role of contemporary coevolution, the structure of interaction networks, the effects of genetic constraints on community level patterns, feedbacks with ecosystem processes and the macroevolutionary history of interacting taxa. However, developing models and statistical methods to explore these areas leads to challenging technical problems. I use my background in mathematics and intuition gained from studying plant-pollinator interactions to confront these challenges and contribute to collaborations with evolutionary biologists, ecologists and mathematicians.
</b>
</p>

### Current position

<p align=justify style="background-color:#2E2E2E;
border-radius:4px;
font-size:16px;
padding:15px;
margin:5px;"><b>
I am currently in search of a postdoctoral research position with a long-term goal of starting a lab focused on developing eco-evolutionary methods to infer the processes structuring ecological communities. I earned my PhD at the University of Idaho advised by Professor [Scott Nuismer](https://www.leeef.org) in the summer of 2020. My degree is in Bioinformatics and Computational Biology ([BCB](https://www.uidaho.edu/sci/bcb)), an interdisciplinary PhD program closely affiliated with The Institute for Bioinformatics and Evolutionary Studies ([IBEST](http://www.ibest.uidaho.edu/)). My dissertation committee consists of Professors [Luke Harmon](https://scholar.google.com/citations?hl=en&user=nsA3IsgAAAAJ&view_op=list_works), [Paul Hohenlohe](https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=QPMrd8EAAAAJ&hl=en) and [Steve Krone](https://www.webpages.uidaho.edu/~krone/res.html). My CV is available [here](bob_week.pdf).
</b>
</p>

### PhD work

<p align=justify style="background-color:#2E2E2E;
border-radius:4px;
font-size:16px;
padding:15px;
margin:5px;"><b>
My dissertation contributes to a coevolutionary theory of community ecology with a focus on plant-pollinator networks. Using Diffusion Limits of Measure-Valued Branching Processes (e.g., [super-Brownian motion](http://www.math.ubc.ca/~perkins/superbrownianmotionandcriticalspatialsystems.pdf)), my work provides a stochastic synthesis of quantitative genetics and population ecology (preprint [here](https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2020.07.28.226001v1)). I apply this synthetic approach to derive models of coevolving communities that track the stochastic dynamics of abundance and quantitative traits. Using this framework, I have investigated the evolutionary dissolution of mutualisms experiencing coevolutionary arms races (ms in prep) and have developed a Maximum Likelihood method to infer the strength of coevolution between pairs of species using spatially structured phenotypic data ([Week & Nuismer 2019](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/ele.13231)). My defense was recorded and can be viewed [here](https://youtu.be/HpvkIWx_3Ds). Interactive slides are available [here](https://bobweek.github.io/coevolutionary-community-ecology.html).
</b>
</p>
